calendar.matters:
MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
Off Calendar Jun-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Randall Whitney, Esq. 925-988-0804 for Petitioner Pro Per.
James Hanlon, Esq. 925-210-2810 of Glen & Finley for DEFENDANT/RESPONDENT METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views and the email received yesterday. Plaintiff Argues. The Court denies Plaintiff's oral request for leave to amend.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT METROPOLITAN TRANSPORTATION COMMISSION's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Metropolitan Transportation Commission's (MTC) motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted. The California State Legislature imposed the bridge toll increase in the San Francisco Bay Area by passing SB 595, of which the Court takes judicial notice. (RJN, Exh. C; see also Sts. & Hy. Code 30916, 30923.) Under Article XIIIC of the California Constitution, "impose" means "enact" or "establish" and does not include the collection of the alleged "tax." (See Cal. Cannabis Coalition v. City of Upland (2017) 3 Cal.5th 924, 944 n.17 ["'impose' most plausibly means to establish or enact, and article XIII C, section 2 applies only if it is the local government doing so."]; Webb v. City of Riverside (2018) 23 Cal.App.5th 244, 258 ["A tax is imposed when first enacted."].) By statute, MTC's responsibility merely consists of overseeing transportation projects funded by the bridge toll revenues after they are collected and preparing a summary of the expenditure plan under Regional Measure 3. (Sts. & Hy. Code 30914.7, 30923(d).) Because the Legislature and not MTC imposed the tax, Article XIIIC of the California Constitution is inapplicable as the Legislature is not a local government. (See Cal. Const. art. XIIIC, 1(e) [defining tax as "any levy, charge, or exaction of any kind imposed by a local government"] [emphasis added].) Thus, Petitioner Randall Whitney's first three causes of action-predicated on this article's two-thirds vote requirement-must fail.

Petitioner's conflation of MTC with the Bay Area Toll Authority (BATA) does nothing to aid his argument because even if MTC were charged with BATA's duties, BATA's discretion in implementing the toll increase was limited to determining the amount of the increase, deciding when the election would be held, and authoring the ballot question. (Sts. & Hy. Code 30916(c)(1), 30923(c).) Despite these limited areas of discretion, BATA was required to carry out the increase enacted by the Legislature; BATA did not itself impose the increase. (See id. 30916(c)(1) ["[BATA] shall increase the base toll rate for vehicles crossing the bridges'"] [emphasis added].)(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
Off Calendar May-23-2019 Continued To Jun-11-19 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Randall Worsley Whitney, Esq., Pacific Thomas Capital, Telephone 925-988-0804, appearing on his own behalf as Pro Per/Pro Se Plaintiff;
James M. Hanlon, Jr., Glynn & Finley, LLP, Telepone 925-945-1975, appearing for Defendant Metropolitan Transporation Commision;

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
Off Calendar Dec-18-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Randall C. Whitney, Telephone 415-716-9898, appearing for Trustee, Randall C. Whitney (CourtCall);
No other appearance.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Respondent Metropolitan Transportation Commission's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the first three causes of action in the petition filed by Randall Whitney is denied as to all three causes of action. The recent decision of Templo v. State of California (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 730 requires denial of MTC's motion. The plaintiffs in Templo alleged a claim that a state statute was unconstitutional because it constituted an improper "tax" and was not enacted by the two-thirds vote required by Article XIII of the California Constituiton and named the State of California as a defendant on that claim. The State filed a motion for judgment on the pleadings as to that claim on the grounds that the state agency that administers the program established by the statute, not the State, is the proper defendant on the claim. In holding that the State was not the proper defendant and the state agency who administered the statute was the proper defendant, the court of appeal, quoting a California Supreme Court case, stated that "in cases challenging the constitutionality of a statute, the interest of the Legislature or the Governor 'is not of the immediacy and directness requisite to party status.' Rather, state and local agencies with 'direct instituional interest' are the proper defendants." (214 Cal. App. 4th at 736, quoting Serrano v. Priest (1976) 18 Cal. 3d 728, 752 (internal citation omitted)). Per Templo, MTC, which acknowledges that it is tasked with overseeing and funding the programs resulting from the increased revenue from SB595 and RM3, is a proper defendant on Mr. Whitney's claims seeking to establish the invalidity of SB595 and RM3. Prevailing party to prepare form of order pursuant to CRC 3.1312

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/HEK)

Petitioner Randall Whitney'S Demurrer To The Amended Answer Of Respondent Metropolitan Transportation Commission
Off Calendar Dec-18-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Randall C. Whitney, Telephone 415-716-9898, appearing for Trustee, Randall C. Whitney, (CourtCall)
No other appearance.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Petitioner Randall Whitney's demurrer to all five affirmative defenses alleged in the amended answer filed by respondent Metropolitan Transportation Commission is overruled as to all five affirmative defenses. All five affirmative defenses are adequately alleged because it is at least theoretically possible that MTC could prevail on any of them and each of them is a potential bar to some or all of the relief sought by Mr. Whitney. Order is signed.
